questions.optionB: Bilateral, pale, smooth, insensitive to touch, and pedunculated (result from allergic/chronic inflammation of ethmoidal sinus mucosa)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Ethmoidal polyps = bilateral, pale, insensitive, allergy-related. Why correct: Nasal polyps from ethmoidal sinuses: bilateral, multiple, pale/glistening, smooth, grape-like, insensitive (do NOT bleed on touch). Associated with: nasal allergy, asthma, aspirin sensitivity (Samter's triad = nasal polyps + asthma + aspirin sensitivity). Why A wrong: UNILATERAL polyp → must rule out antrochoanal polyp (from maxillary sinus, single, extends to choana) or malignancy. Why C wrong: Polyps that are red/vascular → could be inverted papilloma or malignancy, needs biopsy. Why D wrong: Insensitive (not painful). Clinical relevance: Treatment: topical/systemic steroids first-line. Surgery (FESS) if medical treatment fails. Antrochoanal polyp: always needs surgery. Exam trap: UNILATERAL nasal mass → biopsy to rule out malignancy (especially in elderly). BILATERAL = usually ethmoidal (benign). Memory aid: 'Ethmoidal polyps = Both sides, Both Pale, Both Painless.' Reference: Dhingra – Nose.
questions.conceptTag: Ethmoidal polyps bilateral pale insensitive Samter
